title: Recursive Fibonacci Complexity

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Recursive Fibonacci int Fib(int n) { if (n<=0) return 0; if (n==1) return 1; return Fib(n-1) + Fib(n-2); }. If Fib(n) takes 100 seconds, how long does Fib(n+1) take?
rules: Running time satisfies T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + 1 (same recurrence as F_n).
Closed form: F_n = (phi^n - psi^n) / sqrt(5) with phi = (1 + sqrt(5))/2 ~ 1.618.
Iterative DP: O(n). Matrix exponentiation [[1,1],[1,0]]^n: O(log n).
setup.question: Compute T(n+1)/T(n) and identify two algorithms that beat the recursive blow-up.

learningObjectives: Show that T(n) ~ phi^n for the recursive version.
Identify O(n) DP and O(log n) matrix exponentiation as fixes.
Connect to the broader principle: memoize overlapping subproblems.

stages.label: Time ratio
stages.prompt: T(n) ~ phi^n with phi ~ 1.618. If Fib(n) takes 100s, how long does Fib(n+1) take?
stages.explanation: T(n+1)/T(n) -> phi as n grows. So Fib(n+1) takes ~ 1.618 * 100 ~ 162 seconds.

stages.label: Fix to O(n)
stages.prompt: How do you reduce to O(n) without changing the answer?

options.text: Iterative DP: keep two variables a = F_{i-1}, b = F_i, and update (a, b) -> (b, a + b). Theta(n) time, Theta(1) space.
Memoize and look up; same exponential time.
Use Binet's formula -- but floating-point precision fails for large n.

stages.explanation: Iterative computation of F_0, F_1, ..., F_n with a sliding pair of variables. Theta(n) operations. Memoization also gives Theta(n) but with extra space. Binet works in theory but loses precision for n > ~70.

stages.label: Commit -- O(log n) trick
stages.prompt: How do you get O(log n)?

options.text: Matrix exponentiation: [[1,1],[1,0]]^n = [[F_{n+1}, F_n], [F_n, F_{n-1}]]. Compute the matrix power by repeated squaring -- O(log n) multiplications of 2x2 matrices.

stages.explanation: Repeated squaring: M^n = (M^(n/2))^2 (with adjustment for odd n). T(n) = T(n/2) + Theta(1) -> Theta(log n). For arbitrary-precision integers, the bit-level cost is O(log n * M(n)) where M is multiplication cost.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why is the naive recursion so bad? (It recomputes Fib(k) for the same k an exponential number of times.)
What's the broader principle? (Dynamic programming: memoize overlapping subproblems to avoid recomputation.)
Apply to other recursions: how would you compute the n-th term of a linear recurrence in O(log n)? (Same matrix exp trick.)
